supporting_points: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
Pedestrians distracted by texting look left and right less often and switch, distribute and maintain less visual attention on the traffic environment

title: Factor Focus: Behavioral Factors
section_goal: Synthesize the behavioral factors evidence cluster across 16 studies, highlighting 0 gap signals and 0 contradiction signals. Use Behavioral Factors, Traffic Factors as the organizing lens across 6 matrix rows.

blocks.purpose: Frame Factor Focus: Behavioral Factors around Behavioral Factors, Traffic Factors and clarify why this evidence cluster matters.
blocks.intended_purpose: Frame Factor Focus: Behavioral Factors around Behavioral Factors, Traffic Factors and clarify why this evidence cluster matters.

theme_refs.label: Behavioral Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: behavioral_factors appears in 16 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

theme_refs.label: Traffic Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: traffic_factors appears in 14 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: This paper examines pedestrians' self-reported violating crossing behavior intentions by applying the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB). The study extends TPB by adding new factors including descriptive norm, perceived risk and conformity tendency to evaluate their respective impacts on pedestrians' behavior intentions. The survey used a scenario where pedestrians crossed the road violating pedestrian lights at an intersection in Dalian, China.

sentence_plan.directive: Introduce the subsection focus and explain why this evidence cluster matters for the review argument.
Anchor the framing in the strongest relevant evidence, themes, or matrix signals rather than generic setup.
Close with a sentence that narrows toward the next evidence-bearing claim.

blocks.purpose: Anchor the section in 6 extracted claim signal(s) and the strongest matrix evidence.
blocks.intended_purpose: Anchor the section in 6 extracted claim signal(s) and the strongest matrix evidence.

theme_refs.label: Behavioral Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: behavioral_factors appears in 16 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

theme_refs.label: Traffic Factors
theme_refs.synthesis_note: traffic_factors appears in 14 evidence unit(s), with 0 gap signal(s) and 0 contradiction signal(s).

supporting_points: Instrumental attitude was significant in all four models, indicating pedestrians are willing to take risks to cross the road if this behavior is associated with meaningful benefits like saving time or providing convenience
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best predictive utility with the best goodness of fit (CFI=0.996, CMIN/DF=1.235, RMSEA=0.030)
